risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients with LVEF ≤45% will be randomized to control arm (Group 1) or treatment arm (Group 2) in a 1:1 ratio, stratified by country." Comment: The report explicitly states that eligible patients were randomized and describes stratified randomization by country, which is a context clue suggesting a proper random allocation process. Although the exact sequence-generating method is not specified, the description is sufficient to support low risk under the permissive criteria.

Summary: The report describes randomization ("Patients with LVEF ≤45% will be randomized to control arm (Group 1) or treatment arm (Group 2) in a 1:1 ratio, stratified by country"), but it does not state how the allocation sequence was concealed from the investigator enrolling participants. No explicit central allocation, sealed opaque sequentially numbered envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is described. Comment: Because the method of concealment is not reported, foreknowledge of the next assignment cannot be excluded.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomized controlled open-label design" and "Patients in the control group will not undergo any of the described interventions and will not receive the BM-MNC therapy." Comment: The study was explicitly open-label, so participants and treating personnel were not blinded to allocation. Because the intervention involved an invasive bone marrow aspiration and intracoronary infusion, knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect care, co-interventions, reporting, and follow-up behavior across outcomes, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "The defined cardiovascular efficacy endpoints will be adjudicated by an independent Clinical Event Committee blinded to the patient treatment allocation." Comment: The primary outcome is all-cause mortality, an objective outcome usually obtained from records, and the reported cardiovascular efficacy endpoints were assessed by an independent committee blinded to allocation. The paper does not explicitly say who adjudicated all-cause death, but the outcome is hard to influence by knowledge of treatment assignment, so detection bias for this domain is low.

Quote: "With an accrual period of 3 years, total study duration of 5 years and an expected drop-out rate of 10% at 2 years, it is estimated that a total of 3000 patients will be needed to observe the required number of events during the study period." Comment: The report explicitly anticipates only 10% drop-out at 2 years, which is a reasonably low attrition rate. It also states that "The efficacy analyses of this study will be performed on the full analysis set (FAS), including all randomized patients according to their randomized treatment," which supports appropriate handling of missingness. Although this is a study design paper rather than a completed results paper and does not provide observed per-arm dropout numbers, the described follow-up plan and low expected attrition make attrition bias unlikely.
